meta.project: Vitics — Nigeria AI Energy Intelligence Platform
meta.description: Grounded knowledge entries extracted from Nigerian energy policy documents. Focus: what consumers actually experienced vs what policy promised.

documents.title: Rural Electrification Strategy and Implementation Plan (RESIP)
documents.issuer: Federal Ministry of Power, Works and Housing

policy_promises.promise: Electricity access for 75% of the population (rural and urban)
Electricity access for 90% of the population
Add 1 million rural connections and 800MW of generation capacity
policy_promises.note: Interim target set within the document itself

policy_promises.promise: DisCos (Distribution Companies) to add 4 million new connections
policy_promises.note: Obligation under privatization contracts with BPE

policy_promises.promise: Connect 1.1 million additional rural households per year
Establish a transparent, competitive Rural Electrification Fund (REF) for capital subsidies
Rural Electrification Agency (REA) to maintain 6 Zonal offices nationwide
Reliable and affordable electricity for all Nigerians regardless of location
Promote cheaper, cleaner alternatives to kerosene, candles, and fossil fuel generators in rural areas
Subsidies focused on expanding access rather than consumption — benefiting the poorest rural households

admitted_failures.finding: Rural electricity access was approximately 26% in 2016 — no significant improvement from when the Rural Electrification Programme began in 1981
Over 70 million rural Nigerians lacked access to reliable electricity supply
Approximately 1,600 rural electrification projects were abandoned across all states as of 2012; contractors had left sites for more than 5 years
Many completed rural electrification projects could not be put to meaningful use because they had no reliable source of power supply — infrastructure built but non-functional
The Rural Electrification Agency (REA) was suspended in late 2009, stalling strategy development for years
Population growth outpaced new connections — the rate of new household formation exceeded the rate of new electricity connections
DisCos' commercial objectives led them to focus on urban areas, not rural communities, despite contractual rural obligations
Most abandoned projects were grid extension projects. Even when funded, they relied on a national grid that itself lacked reliable generation
The EPSR Act 2005 established the legal framework but contained 'few details on its implementation' — leaving a gap between legislation and action for over a decade
Rural households continued to rely on fuel-wood, kerosene, candles, and fossil fuel generators — the same energy sources the programme was designed to replace since 1981

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Private sector would drive rural electrification through DisCos and new market entrants
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: DisCos focused commercially on urban areas; rural areas remained unserved

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: The REF would provide transparent, competitive capital subsidies from 2015
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: REA was suspended in 2009; REF was not operational; the 2016 document still describes the fund as future intent

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: 4 million DisCo connections by 2017 would expand rural access
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: DisCo commercial incentives directed investment to urban not rural areas

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Grid extension would bring electricity to rural Local Government Areas
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: 1,600 projects abandoned; completed projects had no reliable power supply

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Off-grid and mini-grid solutions would serve remote communities
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: No viable financing ecosystem; high barriers to entry; capacity gaps among developers

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: 75% national electricity access achievable by 2020
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: Rural access rate was still ~26% in 2016, unchanged from 1981

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Rural households would transition from kerosene and fuel-wood to grid or off-grid electricity
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: Rural households continued burning kerosene, candles, and fuel-wood

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: The required 6,000MW of new generation for rural demand could be mobilised
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: 6,000MW exceeded the entire existing capacity of the Nigerian power system at the time

documents.ai_narrative: The 2016 RESIP is both a plan and an inadvertent confession. Nigeria's government admitted in writing that 35 years of rural electrification effort produced near-zero measurable improvement. The programme started in 1981. By 2016, the rural access rate was still approximately 26%. Meanwhile, 1,600 projects sat abandoned nationwide, and the very agency meant to fix the problem — the REA — had been suspended in 2009. The new plan required spending more than the entire capacity of the existing Nigerian power system just to hit its 2020 interim target. Rural consumers in 2016 were still burning firewood and kerosene — the same fuels the 1981 programme was designed to replace.
documents.consumer_experience_summary: Rural Nigerians in 2016 experienced darkness, dependence on expensive and unhealthy alternatives (kerosene, candles, fuel-wood, generators), reduced economic productivity, compromised health and education outcomes, and continued migration to urban areas.

documents.title: Energizing Economies Programme Overview — Upscaling Minigrids for Least Cost and Timely Access to Electricity
documents.category: Commercial/Urban Energy Programme — Baseline Survey and Field Evidence
programme_objectives: Provide safe, reliable and predictable energy to industries and businesses to improve economic output
Create an incentive and working model for private investors in innovative power solutions
Provide street lighting to enable longer and safer business hours

target_sites.name: Ariaria International Market
target_sites.proposed_solution: 5 MW CNG gas fired power plant with independent distribution network and street lighting

target_sites.name: Sabon Gari Market
target_sites.proposed_solution: Stand-alone solar systems (1–2 KW per shop) or 1 MW solar hybrid power plant with street lighting

target_sites.name: Somolu Printing Community
target_sites.note_other: 21 MW is printing equipment — critical productive load
target_sites.proposed_solution: 3–5 MW CNG natural gas fired power plant with independent distribution network and street lighting

target_sites.name: Sura Shopping Complex
target_sites.proposed_solution: Connection to existing gas fired power plant on Lagos Island with independent distribution network and street lighting

status_quo_conditions.condition: 72–84% of traders across all four markets were using alternatives to DisCo power — the majority had effectively abandoned the national grid
Market traders collectively paying over $70,000 per day for self-generated diesel power across the four markets
status_quo_conditions.consumer_impact: direct financial burden on small and micro businesses

status_quo_conditions.condition: 480+ diesel generators documented running in markets — causing noise pollution and HSE risks from ad hoc installations
Old and dangerous overhead distribution lines documented at market sites
Darkness after business hours linked to robbery, harassment, and vandalism — crime directly attributable to lack of reliable street lighting
43–59% of traders stated they would expand their shops or hire more staff if given reliable power — demonstrating suppressed economic growth directly caused by energy failure
Somolu Printing Community required 21 MW for printing equipment alone — Nigeria's printing industry running almost entirely on diesel

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: DisCos would serve commercial and industrial clusters reliably after privatization
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: 72–84% of market traders had abandoned DisCo power and were self-generating with diesel

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Privatization of DisCos would lower energy costs and improve reliability for businesses
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: Businesses paying >$70,000 per day collectively for generator power with zero government subsidy

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Grid-connected electricity would support Nigerian commerce and industry
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: Commercial clusters running 480+ diesel generators as primary power — grid effectively irrelevant

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Urban and peri-urban consumers would benefit from DisCo service improvement
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: Even large commercially active urban markets had been abandoned by DisCos

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Economic growth and job creation would follow improved electricity access
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: 43–59% of traders explicitly holding back expansion and hiring due to energy unreliability

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Safety and quality of life would improve as electricity access expanded
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: Darkness from grid failure directly enabling robbery, harassment, and vandalism

cross_document_links.note: DOC_001 (RESIP 2016) promised DisCos would add 4 million connections by 2017; DOC_002 shows that one year into that deadline, 72–84% of commercial traders had abandoned DisCo power entirely
DOC_001 described affordable electricity for businesses; DOC_002 shows businesses bearing 100% of energy costs through expensive diesel self-generation

documents.ai_narrative: By 2017, the government's own field surveys confirmed what every Nigerian market trader already knew: the grid had effectively ceased to function as a business utility. In four of Nigeria's most economically active commercial clusters — Ariaria, Sabon Gari, Somolu, Sura — between 72% and 84% of traders had given up on DisCo power entirely and were self-generating with diesel. These markets collectively housed over 50,000 shops and were spending more than $70,000 every day just to keep the lights on. The Energizing Economies programme was a tacit government admission: the grid could not be fixed fast enough, so off-grid minigrids would have to substitute for it in Nigeria's economic heartland.
documents.consumer_experience_summary: In 2017, Nigeria's commercial consumers in major urban markets were bearing the full cost of grid failure through daily diesel expenditure, health risks from generator fumes, restricted business hours due to darkness, crime enabled by inadequate lighting, and suppressed economic expansion.
related_documents: REA Energizing Economies Baseline Survey and Energy Audit Report, 2017

documents.title: National Integrated Infrastructure Master Plan (NIIMP) — Revised 2020–2043
documents.issuer: Federal Ministry of Finance, Budget and National Planning
documents.category: National Infrastructure Master Plan — Energy Chapter

what_policy_promised.promise: Increase power generation from 3.5 GW to 7 GW by 2021, 11 GW by 2023, and 25 GW by 2025
Increase electricity access from 40% in 2019 to 75% by 2025
Increase transmission capacity from 5,000 MW to 40,000 MW by 2025 and 470,000 MW by 2043
Increase distribution capacity from 6,000 MW to 36,000 MW by 2025 and 420,000 MW by 2043
Expand 330KV transmission route lines from 5,552 km to 8,000 km by 2025
Invest USD 759 billion in energy infrastructure over the 23-year plan horizon
Invest USD 49.5 billion in energy annually in the first 5 years (2021–2025)
Add 13 GW of off-grid solar power by 2030
Reduce total power losses to global standards by 2026–2043
Privatization of NIPP generation assets to attract private sector and improve supply
Implement the Presidential Power Initiative (PPI): operational grid to 7 GW (Phase 1), 11 GW (Phase 2), 25 GW (Phase 3)

what_document_admitted_was_failing.finding: Nigeria's installed generation capacity was 12,500 MW as of October 2020, but only approximately 3,200 MW was actually being transmitted to consumers — a utilization rate of just 25.6%
what_document_admitted_was_failing.consumer_impact: direct — 74% of installed capacity unavailable to consumers

what_document_admitted_was_failing.finding: Per capita electricity consumption was only 144.52 kWh per year — one of the lowest in the world and lower than Brazil (2,619.96 kWh) and South Africa (2,619.96 kWh)
what_document_admitted_was_failing.consumer_impact: direct — chronic energy deprivation affecting productivity, health, education

what_document_admitted_was_failing.finding: Nigeria is the largest user of oil-fired backup generators in Africa — self-generated electricity (approximately 6,000 MW) was almost three times the amount fed into the national grid
what_document_admitted_was_failing.consumer_impact: direct financial burden — all Nigerians with generators paying full fuel costs with no subsidy

what_document_admitted_was_failing.finding: Electricity access stood at only 45% in 2020 (rural: 36%, urban: 55%) — the RESIP 2016 target of 75% by 2020 was missed by 30 percentage points
An estimated 20 million Nigerian households were without access to electricity as of October 2020
Power sector privatization in 2013 had 'yet to deliver results' seven years later — the document's own words
Total power losses (generation to consumer) estimated at over 50% of generated power — attributable to transmission, distribution losses, vandalism, and pilferage
what_document_admitted_was_failing.consumer_impact: direct — consumers bearing cost of lost electricity through tariffs

what_document_admitted_was_failing.finding: DisCos could not provide meters to consumers — resulting in widespread estimated billing (billing guesswork) rather than actual consumption measurement
what_document_admitted_was_failing.consumer_impact: direct — consumers charged for power not used; disputes unresolvable without meters

what_document_admitted_was_failing.finding: The prevailing electricity tariff (N6–N15.8/kWh) was far below the average cost of production (N23/kWh), creating structural insolvency across GenCos and DisCos and gas supply unpayability
what_document_admitted_was_failing.consumer_impact: indirect — tariff inadequacy caused gas debt, causing gas shortage, causing generation collapse

what_document_admitted_was_failing.finding: Virtually all public sector institutions owed huge backlogs of debt to DisCos — government itself was a primary driver of the sector's financial collapse
The transmission system could only wheel about 5,000 MW — less than 50% of prevailing electricity demand — due to sustained underinvestment
Nigeria fell short of Vision 2020 and ERGP (2017–2020) targets, described as mainly a result of the huge infrastructure deficit in the power sub-sector
Power generation from backup generators surpassed hydro power generation for the entire decade from 2010 to 2020
what_document_admitted_was_failing.consumer_impact: direct — Nigerians self-funding electricity the state should have provided for a full decade

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: 75% electricity access by 2020
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality_2020: Only 45% access achieved — missed by 30 percentage points. Rural access only 36%.

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: 2013 privatization of power sector would deliver results and improve supply
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality_2020: Seven years after privatization, the NIIMP itself states it had 'yet to deliver results'

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Nigeria's installed generation capacity of 12,500 MW would power the economy
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality_2020: Only 3,200 MW actually transmitted — 74% of capacity was non-functional. Several major plants producing zero MW.

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Grid electricity would reduce consumer dependence on expensive backup generators
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality_2020: Self-generated electricity (6,000 MW) was approximately 3x the electricity fed into the national grid. Nigeria became Africa's largest user of backup generators.

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Electricity tariffs would support financially sustainable power sector
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality_2020: Tariffs (N6–N15.8/kWh) were far below production cost (N23/kWh), causing sector-wide insolvency, gas supply debts, and generation collapse — a self-reinforcing failure loop

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Distribution companies would meter and accurately bill consumers
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality_2020: DisCos could not provide meters — consumers billed on estimates, with no recourse

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Power sector investment and reform would raise per capita electricity consumption
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality_2020: Per capita consumption was 144.52 kWh — far below Brazil (2,620 kWh) and South Africa (2,620 kWh), and among the world's lowest

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Public sector institutions would pay electricity bills, sustaining DisCo revenues
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality_2020: Nearly all public sector institutions owed huge backlogs of debt to DisCos — government was itself a primary cause of sector financial collapse

cross_document_links.note: RESIP 2016 (DOC_001) set 75% electricity access target for 2020. NIIMP 2020 (DOC_003) reports actual access was 45% — a 30-percentage-point miss on the same government's own 4-year-old promise.

Energizing Economies 2017 (DOC_002) documented market traders spending $70K/day on generators. NIIMP 2020 (DOC_003) confirms this pattern nationally: 6,000 MW of self-generation vs ~2,000 MW from the grid — Nigeria became Africa's largest generator user.

documents.ai_narrative: By 2020, Nigeria's own National Infrastructure Master Plan told the full story of cumulative failure. Of 12,500 MW of installed generation capacity, only 3,200 MW was reaching consumers — a 74% utilization collapse. Nigerians were generating three times more electricity from their own private generators (6,000 MW) than the national grid was delivering. The country had become Africa's largest user of backup generators, a title no nation aspires to. Per capita electricity consumption sat at 144.52 kWh — the same as a country with almost no power sector at all, compared to Brazil's 2,620 kWh. The 2013 privatization, which was supposed to transform the sector, had 'yet to deliver results' seven years on. And the RESIP 2016 target of 75% electricity access by 2020 was missed by 30 percentage points — national access was only 45%, rural access only 36%. Each policy document in this knowledge base contains a new set of promises. Each subsequent document confirms the previous promises were broken. The NIIMP 2020 breaks this pattern not by delivering on past promises, but by issuing even larger ones — 350 GW by 2043, 100% access — while the foundational problems of gas supply, tariff insolvency, metering, and distribution losses remain unresolved.
documents.consumer_experience_summary: In 2020, the average Nigerian was consuming 144.52 kWh of electricity per year — enough for roughly 12 days of modest usage in a developed country. They were buying their own electricity through generators at unsubsidised fuel prices, paying estimated bills with no meters to verify accuracy, losing power to a grid that transmitted only a quarter of its installed capacity, and watching policy document after policy document promise transformations that did not arrive. Twenty million Nigerian households had no grid access at all. Those who did had power for a fraction of the day.
related_documents: National Renewable Energy and Efficiency Policy (NREEP)
